title: Johnson & Johnson — Relationships
html:
Module 2: The J&J-ZoomRx Relationship

Governing Thought

ZoomRx is J&J's most tenured and deeply embedded customer insights partner — 10+ years, 6 therapeutic areas, preferred vendor status — with a unique ability to connect insights across the enterprise that no competitor can match. This depth of institutional knowledge is the single biggest moat protecting and expanding the relationship.

Partnership Timeline

Year Milestone
2014 Relationship established; early SFEA engagements begin
2015-2017 Expansion into Oncology (Solid Tumor, Hematology); IMBRUVICA Digital Tracking launches
2018-2019 DARZALEX SFTO introduced — first integration of SFEA with prescribing claims at J&J; Immunology TA added
2020-2021 Neuroscience added (INVEGA HCP-Patient Conversations); panel-based recruitment deepens
2022-2023 Rybrevant PET tracking begins; Cardiopulmonary TA onboarded; ZoomRx recognized as "tenured and preferred vendor"
2024-2025 ERLEADA SFTO (GU Oncology); Rybrevant MSL Effectiveness study; AI Dynamic Dialogues piloted
2026 TREMFYA GI Global Proposal ($568K+ across 6 markets); J&J Workshop; IMM PET Takeover; MEE expansion

Key statistic: The partnership now spans 6 TAs (Lung Oncology, GU Oncology, Heme Oncology, Immunology, Neuroscience, Cardiopulmonary) with 25+ distinct project types delivered.

Why J&J Chose ZoomRx (and Keeps Choosing)

J&J's continued investment in ZoomRx rests on three pillars. Each addresses a structural problem that single-engagement vendors cannot solve.

Pillar 1: Consistency

What it means: Standardized metrics and reporting that enable enterprise-level insights while preserving strategic customization at the brand level.

Why it matters at J&J: With 6 TAs running parallel tracking studies, J&J leadership needs to compare performance across brands and teams using the same yardstick. ZoomRx provides that — same scales, same benchmarks, same analytical framework — while still letting each brand team configure what matters to them.

Evidence: The 700M+ industry benchmark data points ZoomRx maintains are the largest in the industry, giving J&J normative context that no internal dataset can replicate.

Pillar 2: Efficiency

What it means: Strong institutional knowledge and battle-tested analytical frameworks that accelerate project initiation and maximize impact per dollar spent.

Why it matters at J&J: J&J teams identified "Context Resets Too Often" as a top pain point — every time a new vendor comes in, weeks are lost re-explaining the brand situation, competitive landscape, and internal dynamics. ZoomRx's continuity eliminates that friction.

Evidence: ZoomRx's 57K+ HCP proprietary panel and target list-based recruitment capability mean faster fielding, higher match rates, and less time explaining methodology to procurement.

Pillar 3: Value Optimization

What it means: A single tracking vendor that can connect the dots across TAs and workstreams, surfacing cross-brand patterns that siloed vendors miss.

Why it matters at J&J: J&J's own teams flagged "Siloed Work, Aggregated Value Expectations" as a core challenge — individual brand teams run separate studies but leadership expects the insights to add up to an enterprise-level view. ZoomRx is the only vendor positioned to deliver that.

Evidence: The "Connecting the Dots" framework (see below) was developed specifically to address this gap and presented at the 2026 J&J Workshop.

The "Connecting the Dots" Framework

ZoomRx diagnosed four structural challenges across J&J's market research ecosystem and mapped solutions to each:

J&J Challenge ZoomRx Solution
Siloed Work, Aggregated Value Expectations — teams want insights that add up at enterprise level Consistency in what we measure across brands and TAs
Context Resets Too Often — each new engagement starts with limited continuity Continuity of learning across time and teams
Teams Move at Different Speeds — some brands are quarterly, others annual Standardization in how insights are structured so comparisons hold regardless of cadence
Scale Increases Complexity Faster Than Visibility — more brands = harder to see patterns Alignment of insights to decisions so complexity translates to clarity, not noise

Proven Impact: Case Studies

1. MM Call Quality Driver Analysis (Heme Oncology)

Challenge: The Multiple Myeloma team needed to understand which dimensions of sales rep interactions actually move the needle on HCP perceptions and prescribing intent. Approach: ZoomRx applied driver analysis to SFEA data, isolating the relative contribution of each call quality attribute. Impact: Identified "Product Knowledge," "Organization," and "Compelling Reason to Prescribe" as the primary drivers — giving the training team a clear prioritization framework for coaching investments rather than trying to improve everything at once.

2. DARZALEX SFTO (Heme Oncology)

Challenge: The DARZALEX team wanted to move beyond attitudinal tracking and quantify the actual prescribing impact of sales force activities. Approach: ZoomRx integrated NPI-level SFEA data with prescribing claims, linking rep effectiveness scores to real-world Rx behavior by physician. Impact: Projected approximately 560 incremental first-line starts from "Dabbler" HCPs and approximately 470 from "Believers" — translating field effectiveness into revenue terms that commercial leadership could act on.

3. IMBRUVICA Digital Tracking (Heme Oncology)

Challenge: The IMBRUVICA team needed to understand the real-world digital promotional exposure HCPs receive across brands, beyond self-reported recall. Approach: ZoomRx deployed its browser extension technology to passively track 116 HCPs' digital interactions over time. Impact: Found that 54% of tracked HCPs received both personal and digital promotion for IMBRUVICA vs. less than 40% for competitors — validating the omnichannel strategy and identifying gaps in competitive digital coverage.

4. Rybrevant MSL Effectiveness (Lung Oncology)

Challenge: The Rybrevant team needed to evaluate whether J&J Lung MSLs were delivering differentiated scientific impact compared to AstraZeneca's MSL force. Approach: ZoomRx fielded an MSL effectiveness study measuring impact on clinical knowledge, quality perceptions, and trial awareness. Impact: Confirmed J&J Lung MSLs deliver strong impact on quality and perceptions vs. AZ. Also surfaced a critical gap: 1 in 3 HCPs still had low awareness of upcoming Rybrevant clinical trials — a direct input to MSL planning.

5. INVEGA HCP-Patient Conversations (Neuroscience)

Challenge: The INVEGA team suspected that HCP-patient conversations were a bottleneck for LAI (long-acting injectable) adoption but had no visibility into what was actually being said. Approach: ZoomRx captured and analyzed 32 real conversations from 15 HCPs, coding for themes, objections, and decision points. Impact: Revealed that novel mechanism of action concerns were the primary factor delaying LAI adoption — a finding that self-reported surveys had not surfaced, giving the brand team a specific message to address.

6. ERLEADA SFTO (GU Oncology)

Challenge: The ERLEADA prostate team needed to connect field activity metrics to in-market prescribing outcomes. Approach: ZoomRx integrated SFEA tracking data with Rx claims to build a tactical optimization model. Impact: Enabled the commercial team to identify which rep behaviors and messages were most predictive of prescribing changes, informing territory-level action plans.

Growth Trajectory & BD Pipeline

The relationship is expanding along three vectors:

Geographic Expansion: TREMFYA GI Global Proposal (March 2026)

The largest single proposal in the relationship's history — $568,655 across three workstreams:

Workstream Budget Markets
SFE (Sales Force Effectiveness) $322,683 US + EU4+UK + Japan + Australia + Brazil
MEE (Medical Effectiveness) $188,472 Same global footprint
Rx Data Integration $57,500 US (claims linkage)

This represents the first time ZoomRx has proposed a fully global program at J&J, moving beyond the US-centric relationship.

TA Expansion: 2026 IMM PET Takeover

ZoomRx is positioning to take over PET (Promotional Effectiveness Tracking) for the broader Immunology franchise — a significant expansion from brand-specific engagements to franchise-level ownership.

Capability Expansion: AI & Advanced Analytics

ZoomRx's AI Dynamic Dialogues (voice-first AI open-ends, 10+ languages), Message Labs, Patient Scribe, and SFTO capabilities are all being surfaced to J&J teams as they explore AI-powered market research. The TREMFYA global proposal in particular creates a natural entry point for AI Dynamic Dialogues in non-English markets.

J&J's AI & Innovation Agenda

What We Know

  • "Utilize AI" is on everyone's GNOs — this is a company-wide mandate, not a niche exploration. Every J&J employee is expected to demonstrate AI adoption.
  • The COE (Center of Excellence) of Market Research is new — J&J has not yet formed a firm viewpoint on how AI should be applied to MR. This is a window of opportunity for ZoomRx to help shape the framework.
  • Heather Kulesik's stated priorities for AI in MR:
    1. Capability that sources relevant data points and suggests questions
    2. Scalability across markets and TAs
    3. Ease of utilization (low friction for non-technical users)
    4. Cost savings

Budget Intelligence

  • Heather estimates MR budget at over $15M. For context, Amgen's MR budget is ~$22M, making $15M likely an underestimate for a company of J&J's scale and TA breadth.
  • Budget pressure makes "cost savings" a real priority, not just a nice-to-have. Position AI capabilities as efficiency multipliers, not premium add-ons.

Internal Tools Already in Play

Tool What It Does Implication for ZoomRx
Microsoft Copilot (premium) Enterprise AI assistant embedded in Office 365 J&J teams are already AI-literate; don't over-explain basics
Signal (on Margo platform) Internal analytics/BI May compete with some reporting functions; differentiate on primary data
EPIC New product BI repository Potential integration target for ZoomRx deliverables
Jesse Heap's AI call plan tool AI-powered call plan optimization Directly adjacent to SFEA/SFTO — ZoomRx should position as a data input to this tool, not a competitor

Competitive AI Threat

ZS Associates has an "MSL and commercial copilot" built into their CRM system. This is the most direct competitive threat — ZS is embedding AI into J&J's daily workflow, which creates stickiness that periodic tracking studies cannot match. See Competitive Threats section below.

Rules of Engagement

Based on stakeholder interactions and proposal feedback, J&J expects the following from vendor partners:

  1. Lead with institutional knowledge, not credentials. J&J stakeholders value that ZoomRx already knows their brands, competitive dynamics, and internal politics. Every interaction should demonstrate continuity, not start from scratch.

  2. Frame recommendations in terms of decisions, not data. J&J's "Connecting the Dots" pain points reveal a team that is drowning in data but starved for decision clarity. Headlines should answer "what should we do" before "what did we find."

  3. Respect the TA team's autonomy while delivering enterprise value. Individual brand teams want to feel ownership of their studies. Cross-TA insights should be offered as "bonus" intelligence, not imposed from above.

  4. AI positioning should emphasize ease and savings. Per Heather Kulesik's priorities, lead with "this makes your existing work faster and cheaper" before "this enables entirely new capabilities."

  5. Named references exist — use them strategically. James Scott (Manager, MR, Solid Tumor Oncology; JScott46@its.jnj.com) and Erin Lennon (Oncology Market Researcher; ELennon1@its.jnj.com) are provided references. These are warm contacts who can validate ZoomRx's track record for new stakeholders.

Competitive Threats to the Relationship

Threat 1: ZS Associates — CRM-Embedded AI

ZS has built an "MSL and commercial copilot" directly into J&J's CRM system. This is the highest-priority threat because:

  • It creates daily-use stickiness that quarterly tracking cannot match
  • It positions ZS as the "always-on" insights partner vs. ZoomRx's periodic cadence
  • Jesse Heap's AI call plan effort may pull ZS deeper into territory traditionally served by ZoomRx's SFTO

Mitigation: Position ZoomRx's primary data (SFEA, MEE, panel) as the upstream intelligence that feeds tools like ZS's copilot. ZoomRx generates the insights; CRM tools operationalize them. This is complementary, not competitive — but only if ZoomRx actively builds the integration narrative.

Threat 2: Katie Szelc's AI Exploration

J&J's market research COE is new and actively exploring AI capabilities. Katie Szelc and others are evaluating what AI can do for MR. If ZoomRx is not in the room for these conversations, another vendor will define the framework.

Mitigation: Proactively demo AI Dynamic Dialogues and Patient Scribe to the COE. Frame ZoomRx as the partner that already has production-grade AI tools, not a vendor that is "exploring AI."

Threat 3: Budget Pressure and Vendor Consolidation

With MR budgets under scrutiny ($15M+ but likely pressured), J&J may look to consolidate vendors. This could work for ZoomRx (as the preferred vendor, consolidation benefits the incumbent) or against ZoomRx (if a cheaper alternative emerges for specific workstreams).

Mitigation: The TREMFYA global proposal and IMM PET Takeover are the right plays — they expand ZoomRx's footprint in ways that make consolidation toward ZoomRx the path of least resistance.

Key Takeaway for New Principals

The J&J relationship is not just large — it is structurally defensible. Ten years of institutional knowledge, cross-TA visibility, and preferred vendor status create switching costs that competitors cannot easily overcome. The principal's job is not to win J&J — it is already won. The job is to deepen the embed, expand into new TAs and geographies, and ensure ZoomRx is positioned as J&J's AI-era insights partner before someone else claims that role.

Back to Accounts Hub | Home
